did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (ConvNeXt Tiny and Custom ConvNeXt), and Knowledge Distillation (KD) techniques (including standard KD and Correlation Congruence for Knowledge Distillation) to perform melanoma depth classification in dermatological imaging. It compares models distilled from a general-purpose dataset (ImageNet) versus a dermatology-specific dataset (SAS).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing two knowledge distillation strategies (ImageNet vs. dermatology-specific SAS dataset) for melanoma depth classification. It details specific methodologies (ConvNeXt models, CCKD), datasets (1000 images from four hospitals), and quantitative performance metrics (Accuracy, F1 Score, AUC, Specificity) across different validation sets, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Domain-Specific Knowledge Distillation for Melanoma Depth in Dermatological Imaging
journal: 2025 6th International Conference in Electronic Engineering & Information Technology (EEITE)
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of domain-specific knowledge distillation on melanoma depth classification using ConvNeXt models. The researchers compared a student model distilled from a general-purpose ImageNet teacher against one distilled from a dermatology-specific dataset (SAS) containing 170,000 unlabeled images. Evaluation was performed on a private dataset of 1,000 images from four hospitals, assessing performance via classification metrics, feature visualization (UMAP, t-SNE), and robustness to image perturbations. Results indicated that while ImageNet-distilled models achieved slightly higher accuracy and AUC, the dermatology-specific model produced more structured feature representations and demonstrated superior robustness to brightness and blur variations. The findings suggest that domain-specific distillation enhances model interpretability and resilience to real-world clinical image variations.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on classifying the depth of invasion (Breslow thickness) of melanoma lesions into two categories: deep and superficial. The dataset consists of 1,000 images from four hospitals, and the classification task is binary (deep vs. superficial).

all_task_types.target: Melanoma depth (Breslow thickness) categorized as deep or superficial
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of melanoma depth into two discrete categories: 'deep' and 'superficial'. The study evaluates classification metrics including Accuracy, F1 Score, Recall, AUC, and Specificity. Although Breslow thickness is a continuous histopathological metric, the AI task is explicitly defined as classifying the depth into these two classes.

datasets_used.name: SAS Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermatology-specific dataset used as the teacher model for domain-specific knowledge distillation. It contains 170,000 unlabeled images. The dataset was supported by Amalia Serrano Gotarredona, Francisca Silva Clavería, and Tomás Toledo-Pastrana.

use.training: Used as the teacher model for domain-specific knowledge distillation to train the student model.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A general-purpose dataset used as the teacher model for standard knowledge distillation to compare against the dermatology-specific SAS dataset.

use.training: Used as the teacher model for standard knowledge distillation.

datasets_used.name: Private Hospital Dataset 1
datasets_used.description: A private prospective dataset of dermatological images from Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena (HUVM) and Hospital Universitario Virgen del Rocío (HUVR). Used for evaluation of the student models.

use.validation: Used for validation loss and metric calculation (Accuracy, F1, Recall, AUC, Specificity).
use.testing: Used for testing model performance under image perturbations (blur and brightness).

datasets_used.name: Private Hospital Dataset 2
datasets_used.description: A private prospective dataset of dermatological images from Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena (HUVM) and Hospital Universitario Virgen del Rocío (HUVR). Used for evaluation of the student models.

use.validation: Used for validation loss and metric calculation (Accuracy, F1, Recall, AUC, Specificity).
use.testing: Used for testing model performance under image perturbations (blur and brightness).

datasets_used.name: Mixed Dataset
datasets_used.description: A previously used mixed dataset mentioned for comparison of distillation strategies, though specific details on its composition or size are not provided in the context.

use.testing: Used for comparative analysis of distillation strategies.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the central tendency and variability of performance metrics (Train Loss, Validation Loss, Accuracy, F1 Score, Recall, AUC, Specificity) across different distillation strategies (SAS vs. ImageNet) and datasets.
To minimize the discrepancy between correlation matrices computed from feature embeddings of teacher and student networks, capturing inter-instance correlations during the distillation process.
To reduce high-dimensional feature space to two dimensions for visualizing clustering behavior and separation of melanoma depth classes (deep vs. superficial).
To generate feature map visualizations highlighting regions of input images contributing most to model predictions, assessing interpretability and focus on lesion-specific features.
To evaluate model resilience by measuring the change in F1 score under controlled image degradations (increasing blur and brightness levels).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the SAS-distilled and ImageNet-distilled models were made using descriptive statistics (Mean ± Standard Deviation) for metrics such as Accuracy, F1 Score, AUC, and Specificity, along with qualitative observations of robustness and feature visualization.

Mixed Dataset (General Evaluation).description: Evaluation on a previously used mixed dataset for comparative analysis of distillation strategies.
Mixed Dataset (General Evaluation).notes: ImageNet-distilled models achieved lower train/validation losses and slightly better classification performance (Accuracy, F1, AUC). SAS-distilled models showed more structured feature representations in UMAP/t-SNE but clusters were influenced by hospital-specific characteristics.

Private Hospital 1.description: Prospective dataset from Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena and Hospital Universitario Virgen del Rocío.
Private Hospital 1.notes: SAS distillation yielded competitive performance, particularly in validation loss and specificity. ImageNet distillation achieved slightly better AUC and F1 scores in this specific split, though SAS models demonstrated superior robustness to blur and brightness perturbations.

Private Hospital 2.description: Prospective dataset from Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena and Hospital Universitario Virgen del Rocío.
Private Hospital 2.notes: ImageNet distillation consistently underperformed compared to SAS distillation in some aspects but achieved better specificity. SAS distillation showed a more gradual decline in performance under brightness variations.

Robustness to Perturbations.description: Evaluation of F1 score under increasing levels of image blur and brightness.

SAS Distilled.Blur Robustness: Maintains relatively stable F1 score as blur level increases.
SAS Distilled.Brightness Robustness: Shows a more gradual decline in F1 score as brightness increases.

ImageNet Distilled.Blur Robustness: Suffers a steep decline in performance as blur increases.
ImageNet Distilled.Brightness Robustness: Undergoes a sharp decline in performance, particularly at higher brightness levels.

Robustness to Perturbations.notes: SAS-distilled models demonstrate greater resilience to real-world image variations (blur and brightness) compared to ImageNet-distilled models.

architecture_used.pretraining: Both teacher and student backbones were pre-trained on ImageNet. The student model was then distilled using either ImageNet (standard KD) or a dermatology-specific dataset (SAS) using Correlation Congruence for Knowledge Distillation (CCKD).

architecture_details.distillation_method: Standard KD (KL divergence) for ImageNet teacher; Correlation Congruence for Knowledge Distillation (CCKD) for SAS teacher
architecture_details.backbone_freezing: Student backbone frozen during training; only classifier trained

classification_or_output_head.details: Follows the backbone; trained while backbone was frozen; no fine-tuning of feature extractor performed

preprocessing_techniques: Image perturbations applied for robustness testing: controlled image blur and brightness transformations
Minimal transformations applied to ensure computational constraints were met while preserving image integrity

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Controlled image blur and brightness transformations were applied to evaluate model robustness under real-world variations. These were used as perturbations for testing rather than training augmentation, with minimal transformations applied to ensure computational constraints were met while preserving image integrity.

conclusions: While ImageNet-distilled models achieved slightly better classification performance in terms of accuracy and AUC, dermatology-specific distillation (using the SAS dataset) led to more structured feature representations.

Models trained with domain-specific knowledge distillation demonstrated enhanced resilience to real-world image variations, such as blur and brightness changes, compared to those distilled from ImageNet.

The structured feature clusters in the SAS-distilled model were strongly influenced by hospital-specific characteristics, suggesting it captures dataset-specific variations effectively but potentially limiting generalization to unseen hospital datasets.
The SAS-distilled model focused more on lesion-specific features in Grad-CAM visualizations, whereas the ImageNet-distilled model showed broader activation including non-lesion regions.
Future work should explore hybrid distillation techniques and domain adaptation strategies to balance domain specificity with cross-domain generalization.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small private prospective dataset of only 1,000 images from two hospitals (HUVM and HUVR), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the findings.

Feature visualization (UMAP/t-SNE) indicates that the dermatology-specific (SAS) model's clustering is strongly influenced by hospital-specific characteristics rather than purely task-relevant features, suggesting potential overfitting to dataset-specific artifacts.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons rely on descriptive statistics (Mean ± Std Dev), making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the observed differences.

The ImageNet-distilled model achieved slightly better accuracy and AUC on the mixed dataset, challenging the assumption that domain-specific distillation always yields superior classification metrics.

The SAS teacher dataset used for distillation contains 170,000 unlabeled images, raising questions about the quality and relevance of the pseudo-labels or features used in the Correlation Congruence for Knowledge Distillation (CCKD) process.

The student model architecture (Custom ConvNeXt) has significantly fewer parameters (396,720) than the teacher (ConvNeXt Tiny, 28,589,128), which may limit its capacity to fully capture complex melanoma depth features compared to larger models.

Robustness tests showed that while SAS-distilled models were more resilient to blur and brightness perturbations, the ImageNet-distilled model suffered sharp declines, indicating that domain specificity improves robustness to real-world image variations but may not improve raw classification accuracy.

The study does not address potential data leakage or bias from the source of the SAS dataset, which could propagate biases into the student model if the source data is not representative of the target clinical population.
